darin@book
title: darin@book
Gate realtime integration tests behind slow-tests
description:
**Problem**
- Some integration tests spawn the daemon, run load generators, and use time-based sleeps without being gated by the slow-tests feature (e.g., tests/integration/daemon/admin_status.rs, tests/integration/daemon/subscribe.rs).
- The test design guideline says slow tests should be opt-in; these can slow the default suite and introduce timing flakiness.

**Files**
- tests/integration/daemon/admin_status.rs
- tests/integration/daemon/subscribe.rs
- any other realtime integration tests that start daemons or sleep
design: Audit integration tests that spawn the daemon or rely on time-based sleeps; gate them with #[cfg(feature = "slow-tests")] (file-level or test-level) or split fast/slow variants. Keep a small smoke test (if needed) in the default suite that asserts basic wiring without sleeps. Ensure docs/justfile already reference slow-tests.
acceptance_criteria: - [ ] Heavy realtime integration tests are opt-in via slow-tests.\n- [ ] Default cargo test remains fast and deterministic.\n- [ ] Any remaining ungated tests that spawn the daemon are intentionally justified in comments.

description: darin@book
title: darin@book
Realtime: stateright model coverage
description:
**Problem**
The stateright model exists but is not validated against production invariants. We have no model-checked coverage for realtime edge cases, which limits confidence in protocol correctness.
design:
**Design**
- Prefer running Stateright over production code paths: reuse core realtime types/logic directly (via shared modules/feature flags) so the model is not a hand-written fork.
- If direct reuse is impossible, add an automated sync mechanism (e.g., codegen or compile-time structure tests) that fails on drift between model and production types/state transitions.
- Invariants should be asserted against the actual implementation of event apply/WAL/replication state, not a simplified model.
acceptance_criteria:
**Acceptance**
- [ ] Model checker uses production code paths or an automated sync mechanism that prevents drift.
- [ ] Invariants (watermarks monotonicity, no event loss, replay idempotency, durability receipts) are checked against the real implementation.
- [ ] CI/slow-test target runs the model (bounded) and fails on counterexamples with actionable traces.

description: darin@book
title: darin@book
Repl rig wait_for_show uses CLI polling; switch to IPC read gating
description: tests/integration/fixtures/repl_rig.rs wait_for_show spawns bd show in a poll loop. Use IpcClient Request::Show with ReadConsistency { require_min_seen, wait_timeout_ms } based on create receipts (min_seen) to block once instead of sleep/poll. Should reduce E2E repl test time + flake risk.
darin@book
title: darin@book

IPC subscribe WAL backfill for require_min_seen

**Problem**
Subscribe streams hot-cache/live events only; it does not backfill WAL ranges when require_min_seen lags current applied.

**Design**
- Compute per-origin backfill ranges from require_min_seen to current applied for the subscribed namespace.
- Use WalRangeReader to read bounded frames (max_event_batch_bytes/events).
- Stream backfill before live subscription; abort with explicit error if range missing/corrupt.

**Acceptance**
- [ ] Backfill frames are streamed before live events when require_min_seen is behind.
- [ ] Backfill is bounded by max batch limits.
- [ ] Error mapping matches REALTIME_ERRORS (corruption/bootstrap_required).
- [ ] Tests cover backfill planning and error paths.

**Files:** src/daemon/server.rs, src/daemon/repl/runtime.rs, src/daemon/ipc.rs, src/api/mod.rs

description: darin@book
title: darin@book
Seal OrSetValue to enforce deterministic collision bytes
description:
**Problem**
`OrSetValue` is a public trait that requires a deterministic byte encoding for collision hashing, but nothing in the type system enforces determinism. External implementations can accidentally use non‑deterministic encodings (iteration over HashMap, randomized ordering, etc.), breaking CRDT determinism in subtle ways. This violates the “types should mirror your domain’s actual algebra” and “types tell the truth” principles.

Key references:
- `crates/beads-core/src/orset.rs:24` — `OrSetValue` is public and unconstrained.

Severity: non-deterministic merges and inconsistent CRDT state if a consumer implements `OrSetValue` incorrectly.
design:
**Design**
Seal or constrain `OrSetValue` so only known-deterministic implementations are possible.

Option A (sealed trait):
- Add a private `sealed` module with a `Sealed` trait.
- `pub trait OrSetValue: sealed::Sealed + Ord + Clone { ... }`.
- Implement `Sealed` only for internal types (String, DepKey, Label, etc.) and document how to add new types internally.

Option B (newtype wrapper):
- Use `Deterministic<T>` wrapper that only exists for vetted types; `OrSetValue` is implemented for `Deterministic<T>` only.

Option C (constructor-enforced bytes):
- Replace `collision_bytes` with a canonical encoder function supplied at construction of the OR-Set, so determinism is localized.

Given repo philosophy, Option A is simplest and preserves API safety.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] External crates cannot implement `OrSetValue` directly.
- [ ] All existing internal implementations continue to work.
- [ ] Documentation/comments explain how to add new deterministic value types internally.
- [ ] CRDT merge determinism is enforced by type boundaries, not conventions.

description: darin@darins-Mac-Studio-2.local
title: darin@darins-Mac-Studio-2.local
Separate protocol error codes from legacy CLI codes
description:
**Problem**
- `core::ErrorCode` mixes realtime protocol codes with legacy CLI/IPC codes, obscuring stability boundaries.
- It is unclear which codes are normative vs compatibility only.

**Files:**
- src/core/error.rs
- REALTIME_ERRORS.md
- CLI_SPEC.md (if needed)
- src/daemon/ipc.rs (mapping)
design:
- Introduce `ProtocolErrorCode` (realtime/replication/IPC stable) and `LegacyErrorCode`.
- Wrap them in `ErrorCode` or introduce `ErrorCode::Protocol/Legacy`.
- Preserve current string values for compatibility; parsing should accept legacy names.
- Update docs to state which codes are protocol-stable vs legacy.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Protocol vs legacy codes are clearly separated in code and docs.
- [ ] Existing serialized strings remain accepted and produced.
- [ ] Tests cover parsing/serialization for both sets.

description: darin@book
title: darin@book
Sync push lockfile errors should be retryable
description: During sync retry tests, push_update_reference returns 'failed to lock file' which is currently treated as PushRejected instead of NonFastForward. Treat lockfile failures as retryable so sync_with_retry can retry.
darin@book
title: darin@book
Centralize IPC request metadata extraction
description:
**Problem**
IPC request metadata is extracted in multiple places with large, partially overlapping matches:
- `crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/server.rs` has `RequestContext::from_request()` for span metadata and `read_gate_request()` for read gating.
- `crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/coord.rs` has the big request dispatch match that re-states which ops are mutation/read/repo-only.
This duplication makes it easy to forget to update one path when new ops are added, causing silent drift in logging or read-gate coverage.

**Design**
After the ctx/payload refactor (bd-ebhh), introduce a single request metadata extraction path and route all span + read-gate logic through it.

1) Add a shared metadata carrier:
- `RequestInfo` (or `RequestExt`) with fields:
  - `op: &'static str` (request type string for tracing)
  - `repo: Option<&Path>`
  - `namespace: Option<&str>`
  - `actor_id: Option<&str>`
  - `client_request_id: Option<&str>`
  - `read: Option<&ReadConsistency>`
- Implement `Request::info(&self) -> RequestInfo<'_>` using a **single match** on `Request` variants.
  - Mutations use `MutationCtx` fields.
  - Reads use `ReadCtx` fields.
  - Repo-only ops use `RepoCtx` with no namespace/read.
  - Admin flush/checkpoint read namespace from payload (not `ReadCtx`).
  - `Ping`/`Shutdown` return `None` repo.

2) Add convenience methods on `Request`:
- `read_gate(&self) -> Option<ReadGateRequest>` built from `RequestInfo` (repo + read), to fully replace the current `read_gate_request()` match.
- (Optional) `request_type(&self) -> &'static str` if it helps keep spans simple.

3) Update server span + read gate logic:
- Replace `RequestContext::from_request()` usage with `request.info()`.
- Delete `RequestContext` struct entirely if no longer needed.
- Replace `read_gate_request()` with the new `Request::read_gate()`.
- Keep `read_consistency_tag()` and its semantics intact, but feed it from `RequestInfo.read`.

4) Ensure the op names used in spans are **exactly** the current names (e.g., "add_labels", "admin_reload_limits").

**Design Notes**
- This is strictly a refactor: no wire format or behavior changes.
- The single match should live in one place. Prefer `beads-surface` if it’s useful across crates, or a daemon-only extension (e.g., `crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/ipc/request_info.rs`) if we want to keep it internal.
- Pay special attention to Admin ops with namespace payloads and to read-gated operations: the read gate coverage must remain identical.

**Files**
- `crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/server.rs`
- `crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/ipc/mod.rs` (if adding extension helpers)
- `crates/beads-surface/src/ipc/types.rs` (if implementing `Request::info` there)
- `crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/server.rs` tests (span/read-gate coverage)
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] `Request::info()` (or equivalent) exists and is the **only** match that maps requests → repo/namespace/actor/read/op.
- [ ] `RequestContext::from_request()` and `read_gate_request()` are removed from `crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/server.rs`.
- [ ] Tracing spans include the same fields/values as before for all ops.
- [ ] Read gate behavior is unchanged for all ops.
- [ ] `cargo test` passes.

description: darin@darins-Mac-Studio-2.local
title: darin@darins-Mac-Studio-2.local
Remove duplicate key from DepEdge
description:
**Problem**
`DepKey` is stored both as the map key AND inside `DepEdge.key`:
```rust
deps: BTreeMap<DepKey, DepEdge>

pub struct DepEdge {
    pub key: DepKey,      // <-- duplicates the map key
    pub created: Stamp,
    pub life: Lww<DepLife>,
}
```

This duplication means:
1. Wasted memory
2. Possible key/value mismatch bugs (though unlikely)

**Design**
Remove `key` from `DepEdge`:
```rust
pub struct DepEdge {
    pub created: Stamp,
    pub life: Lww<DepLife>,
}
```

Callers that need the key get it from the map iteration:
```rust
for (key, edge) in &state.deps {
    // key is available here
}
```

**Design Notes**
- Wire format needs to handle this - on serialization, include key; on deserialization, reconstruct
- `DepEdge::new` and `DepEdge::join` signatures change (no longer take/return key)
- Check all usages of `edge.key` and ensure key is available from context

**Acceptance**
- [ ] `DepEdge` no longer contains `key` field
- [ ] Wire format correctly serializes/deserializes (key comes from map, not struct)
- [ ] All `edge.key` usages updated to use key from iteration context
- [ ] `DepEdge::new`/`join` updated
- [ ] Tests pass

**Files:** src/core/dep.rs, src/git/wire.rs, src/daemon/executor.rs
darin@book
title: darin@book
Repl session typestate + streaming gating
description:
**Problem**
The replication session state machine is enforced by runtime checks and shadow state outside the session. The compiler cannot prevent illegal transitions or misuse.

Specific compiler‑blind spots:
- Session phase/role gating is runtime only (`begin_handshake`, `resend_handshake`, `handle_message`). A wrong call sequence compiles and just returns None/error.
- Both inbound and outbound loops track `streaming` as a separate bool (and `accepted_set` out‑of‑band). Once set, it never goes false even if the session transitions to Draining/Closed. This allows sending events/hot‑cache in invalid phases without compile‑time or runtime guardrails.
- Code that depends on negotiated peer info uses `session.peer()` at runtime and falls back to config when missing; this is a smell that the type system doesn’t model “handshake completed” as a state.

Key refs:
- `crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/repl/session.rs:226` (handshake API), `:251` (message handling)
- `crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/repl/manager.rs:414` / `:528` (shadow `streaming` + `accepted_set`)
- `crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/repl/server.rs:484` / `:596` (same issue inbound)

**Impact**
A single refactor or new feature can accidentally send events before handshake or after draining/close. These bugs will compile and only appear at runtime under load. This is correctness‑critical for repl.
design:
**Design (opinionated)**
Make the session phase/role a *type boundary* and collapse shadow state into the session type.

1) Introduce typestated session:
```rust
struct Session<R, P> { /* fields */ }
// R: Inbound | Outbound, P: Connecting | Handshaking | Streaming | Draining | Closed
```
- Each phase exposes only valid operations.
- `begin_handshake(self) -> (Session<Outbound, Handshaking>, SessionAction)`
- `handle_hello(self, ...) -> Session<Inbound, Streaming>` etc.

2) Encode “peer known” in the type:
- Only `Session<*, Streaming>` (or `StreamingSession` wrapper) exposes `peer()` and `negotiated_max_frame_bytes()`.
- Remove fallbacks on config when peer is None; make it impossible to call those methods pre‑handshake.

3) Remove shadow `streaming` + `accepted_set`:
- The loop should match on the session typestate.
- `accepted_set` is derived from `StreamingSession`’s peer data, not a separately mutated local variable.
- Event send/hot‑cache methods accept `&StreamingSession` only.

4) Phase transitions are explicit:
- `SessionAction` can include a typed next‑state or return `(Session<NewState>, Vec<Action>)` to make transitions explicit and compiler‑checked.

This turns invalid phase usage into compile‑time errors.
acceptance_criteria:
**Acceptance**
- [ ] All handshake/streaming operations are type‑gated (cannot compile in the wrong phase/role).
- [ ] `session.peer()`/`negotiated_max_frame_bytes()` are only available after handshake.
- [ ] The repl loops no longer track `streaming` or `accepted_set` separately; they derive from the session type/state.
- [ ] It is impossible to send events or hot‑cache frames unless the session is in Streaming.
- [ ] Tests cover legal transitions and ensure illegal transitions are unrepresentable (compile‑fail or typestate tests).

description: darin@darins-Mac-Studio-2.local
title: darin@darins-Mac-Studio-2.local
Roster/epoch change e2e for replication
description:
**Problem**
We do not test dynamic replica roster changes or store_epoch bumps. These are high-risk paths for long-running deployments and upgrades.
design:
**Design**
- Add integration helpers to update roster (and/or replication config) at runtime and trigger a reload via admin command.
- If no admin API exists to bump store_epoch, add one (or document the intended mechanism), then simulate an epoch bump and ensure peers reconnect.
- Add slow e2e that changes roster/epoch mid-flight and asserts continued convergence.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Roster update + reload path exercised in e2e.
- [ ] Store_epoch bump path exercised; peers reconnect without data loss.
- [ ] Replication converges post-change.
- [ ] Tests write only under ./tmp.

description: darin@book
title: darin@book
Integration test failure: subscribe require_min_seen disconnects
description:
**Problem**
subscribe_gates_on_require_min_seen fails in tests/integration/daemon/subscribe.rs:104 with Ipc(Disconnected) during the normal integration run (cargo test --test integration). This indicates the subscription stream closes unexpectedly during require_min_seen gating. The same test passed in a subsequent slow-tests run, suggesting a race or lifecycle issue rather than a deterministic assertion failure.

**Repro**
- cargo test --test integration (failed on 2026-01-15)

**Files**
- tests/integration/daemon/subscribe.rs
- src/daemon/ipc/* (subscribe stream), src/daemon/core.rs (subscribe handling), broadcaster/gating logic
design: Investigate why the IPC subscription stream closes during require_min_seen gating. Add structured logging around subscribe open/close paths and daemon lifecycle. Verify the daemon is started and the subscribe request is not racing daemon startup or shutdown. If the stream closes due to a specific error (gate timeout, overload, unexpected payload), surface that explicitly instead of a bare disconnect. Stabilize the test by ensuring the daemon is ready before subscribing or by retrying transient disconnects if the daemon is still live.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] subscribe_gates_on_require_min_seen passes reliably.
- [ ] Subscribe stream failure modes return a structured error (not just disconnect) when possible.
- [ ] Test setup avoids daemon lifecycle races if needed.

description: darin@book
title: darin@book
Replication Session should encode phase/peer invariants
description:
**Problem**
`Session` tracks `phase: SessionPhase` and `peer: Option<SessionPeer>` separately. Streaming-only handlers (e.g. `handle_events`) can still observe `peer == None` and emit an internal error ("missing peer metadata while handling events"). Invariants like “Streaming implies peer is set” are enforced with runtime checks and duplicated phase guards, which is brittle and easy to regress.

**Files**
- src/daemon/repl/session.rs
design:
**Design**
Introduce a state/typestate representation that encodes invariants:
- Use `enum SessionState { Connecting{...}, Handshaking{...}, Streaming{peer: SessionPeer, ...}, Draining{peer: SessionPeer, ...}, Closed }` (or generic typestates) and move `peer` into the streaming/draining variants.
- Refactor `handle_message` to pattern-match on state; only streaming/draining variants accept EVENTS/ACK/WANT/PING/PONG.
- Remove the internal-error path for missing peer metadata and make it unrepresentable.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] `Session` can’t represent a streaming phase without peer metadata (no runtime `peer == None` checks).
- [ ] `handle_events` no longer emits "missing peer metadata" internal errors.
- [ ] Session tests updated/added to cover state transitions and invariants.

description: darin@book
title: darin@book
Remove global test data dir lock by using thread-local overrides
description:
**Problem**
Unit tests that override data dir grab a global lock (paths::lock_data_dir_for_tests) and serialize across the suite. This blocks parallel test execution and shows up as time spent waiting.

**Design**
Use thread-local DataDirOverride (paths::override_data_dir_for_tests) in unit tests instead of global set_data_dir_for_tests + lock. Update affected tests to use RAII guard. Keep lock-based API for cases that truly need cross-thread overrides.

**Acceptance**
- [ ] Tests no longer use lock_data_dir_for_tests when not needed
- [ ] Data dir override is scoped and does not leak
- [ ] Tests still pass in parallel

**Files**: src/git/checkpoint/cache.rs, src/daemon/store_runtime.rs, src/daemon/core.rs
closed_reason: Switched tests to thread-local override_data_dir_for_tests; removed lock usage and added allow(dead_code) on lock APIs.

description: darin@book
title: darin@book
Add bd binary to nix devshell
description: The nix flake devShell should include the bd binary so hooks like 'bd prime' work inside nix develop. Currently bd is only available after building from source.
darin@dusk
title: darin@dusk
Move some repl_e2e coverage to in-process test_harness
description: Use src/test_harness (ReplicationRig/TestWorld) to cover common replication scenarios (roundtrip, tailnet profile, crash/restart, durability receipts) without spawning daemons or tailnet_proxy. Keep 1-2 daemon E2E tests for IPC/real network, but shift most coverage to fast in-process tests.
darin@book
title: darin@book
Refactor executor.rs to use require_live helpers
closed_reason: Refactored executor.rs to use require_live/require_live_mut with MapLiveError trait. Eliminated all .unwrap() calls on bead lookups.

description: darin@dusk
title: darin@dusk
IPC: parse MutationMeta into typed namespace/durability/actor
description:
**Problem**
`MutationMeta` in `src/daemon/ipc.rs` stores `namespace`, `durability`, `actor_id`, and `client_request_id` as `Option<String>`. These are parsed later (and in multiple places), which makes error handling inconsistent and keeps invalid values alive deep into the realtime pipeline.

**Files**
- src/daemon/ipc.rs
- src/daemon/core.rs (parse_durability)
- src/daemon/mutation_engine.rs
design: Introduce an internal `ParsedMutationMeta` that uses `NamespaceId`, `DurabilityClass`, `ActorId`, and `ClientRequestId`. Parse/validate once at IPC boundary (or in a dedicated conversion layer) and propagate the typed struct through `MutationContext` / executor. Move `parse_durability` into a shared helper so the CLI/IPC use the same parser.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Internal mutation flow uses typed `ParsedMutationMeta` (no raw strings).
- [ ] Invalid namespace/durability/actor/client_request_id values fail at IPC boundary with clear errors.
- [ ] Duplicate parsing logic removed.

description: darin@book
title: darin@book
WAL replay should use typed seq/namespace in tracker + errors
description:
**Problem**
WAL replay code stores sequences as raw `u64` and error variants carry `namespace: String` + `seq: u64` (e.g., `WalReplayError::NonContiguousSeq`, `PrevShaMismatch`, `MissingHead`). `ReplayTracker`/`OriginReplayState` also use raw `u64`, forcing manual `seq == 0` checks and losing invariants that should be enforced by `Seq0`/`Seq1` and `NamespaceId`.

**Files**
- src/daemon/wal/replay.rs
design:
**Design**
- Refactor `ReplayTracker`/`OriginReplayState` to use `Seq0`/`Seq1` types for `max_seq`/`contiguous_seq` and comparisons.
- Update `WalReplayError` fields to use `NamespaceId` and typed seqs (`Seq0`/`Seq1`) instead of `String`/`u64`.
- Adjust error mapping/helpers and tests accordingly.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] WAL replay tracker uses Seq types end-to-end (no raw u64 for seq).
- [ ] WalReplayError variants carry `NamespaceId` + Seq types.
- [ ] Tests updated to cover the new typed errors/invariants.

description: darin@book
title: darin@book
Replica roster must make durability eligibility unrepresentable for observers
description:
**Problem**
`ReplicaEntry` stores `role: ReplicaRole` and `durability_eligible: bool` independently. `ReplicaRoster::validate()` never checks for illegal combinations (e.g. `Observer + durability_eligible=true`). This violates parse-don’t-validate and lets invalid configs enter canonical state.

The `DurabilityCoordinator::eligible_replicas` path trusts the roster; in `ReplicateMode::P2p` an observer marked eligible is counted toward durability, which can report acks that never happened. This is a safety bug and the compiler cannot help.

**Impact**
- False durability availability (observer counted toward k) in P2P mode.
- Bad config persists and is indistinguishable from valid at compile time.
- Future refactors can silently reintroduce eligibility bugs since there is no typed guardrail.

**Files**
- `crates/beads-core/src/replica_roster.rs` (ReplicaEntry/ReplicaRoster parse + validate)
- `crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/durability_coordinator.rs` (eligibility derivation)
design:
**Design**
Make illegal combinations unrepresentable and validate at parse boundary. Keep TOML schema stable.

Option A (preferred):
1) Introduce a typed entry that encodes role + eligibility: 
   ```
   enum ReplicaDurabilityRole {
     Anchor { eligible: bool },
     Peer { eligible: bool },
     Observer,
   }
   ```
2) Replace `ReplicaEntry { role, durability_eligible }` with `ReplicaEntry { role: ReplicaDurabilityRole, ... }`.
3) Create a `ReplicaEntryWire` for serde (role + durability_eligible bool). Implement `TryFrom<ReplicaEntryWire> for ReplicaEntry` that rejects `Observer + eligible=true` (and any future invalid combos).
4) Update `ReplicaRoster::from_toml_str` to parse `ReplicaEntryWire`, then convert to typed entries; errors surface as `ReplicaRosterError::InvalidRoleEligibility` (new error variant).
5) Update `DurabilityCoordinator::eligible_replicas` to use `ReplicaDurabilityRole::eligible()` and `role_allows_policy` on the typed role.

Option B (acceptable if we want to keep `ReplicaRole`):
- Replace bool with `DurabilityEligibility` and make it only constructible for non-observers: `eligibility: Option<DurabilityEligible>` or `NonObserver { role: NonObserverRole, eligibility: DurabilityEligibility }`.
- Provide explicit constructors `ReplicaEntry::anchor(...)`, `::peer(...)`, `::observer(...)` so call sites cannot fabricate invalid states.

**Parse-don’t-validate**
- The only public entry point should be typed constructors / `TryFrom` conversion from wire.
- After parsing, there should be no API that exposes a raw bool.

**Ordering / invariants preserved**
- Preserve deterministic ordering of `ReplicaRoster.replicas` by current ordering semantics.
- Keep TOML fields and on-disk/IPC compatibility unchanged (wire struct handles it).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] It is impossible to construct `Observer + eligible=true` at compile time.
- [ ] `ReplicaRoster::from_toml_str` rejects invalid role/eligibility combos with a clear error.
- [ ] `DurabilityCoordinator::eligible_replicas` uses the typed role/eligibility (no raw bool).
- [ ] Unit tests cover: valid anchor/peer entries, observer ineligible, observer eligible rejected.
- [ ] Ordering of `replicas` remains deterministic and unchanged.
- [ ] `cargo test` passes.

description: darin@darins-Mac-Studio-2.local
title: darin@darins-Mac-Studio-2.local
Stateright consistency testers for read/receipt semantics
description:
**Problem**
We currently hand-write invariants for read/receipt semantics. Stateright provides built-in linearizability/sequential consistency testers we’re not using.

**Goal**
Use Stateright’s consistency testers to validate read/receipt behavior against a sequential spec.
design:
**Design**
1) Define a small sequential spec for receipts/reads (e.g., `Register` or `WriteOnceRegister`).
2) Use `actor::register::RegisterMsg` helpers to record invocations/returns:
   - `RegisterMsg::record_invocations`
   - `RegisterMsg::record_returns`
3) Build an ActorModel where client actors issue reads/writes (or receipt queries), and server actors call production code paths to produce responses.
4) Attach `LinearizabilityTester` (or `SequentialConsistencyTester`) to history type H.
5) Add a property that checks `history.is_consistent()`.

**References**
- `~/.cargo/registry/src/index.crates.io-1949cf8c6b5b557f/stateright-0.31.0/src/semantics/consistency_tester.rs`
- `~/.cargo/registry/src/index.crates.io-1949cf8c6b5b557f/stateright-0.31.0/src/semantics/linearizability.rs`
- `~/.cargo/registry/src/index.crates.io-1949cf8c6b5b557f/stateright-0.31.0/src/semantics/sequential_consistency.rs`
- `~/.cargo/registry/src/index.crates.io-1949cf8c6b5b557f/stateright-0.31.0/src/actor/register.rs`
- Example: `~/.cargo/registry/src/index.crates.io-1949cf8c6b5b557f/stateright-0.31.0/examples/linearizable-register.rs`
acceptance_criteria:
**Acceptance**
- [ ] A model exists that uses `LinearizabilityTester` or `SequentialConsistencyTester` for read/receipt semantics.
- [ ] The model uses `RegisterMsg::record_invocations` + `record_returns` instead of custom history tracking.
- [ ] Consistency property is asserted and passes in the bounded model.

description: darin@book
title: darin@book
BeadPatch.status should be typed (WorkflowStatus)
description:
**Problem**
`src/daemon/ops.rs` defines `BeadPatch.status` as `Patch<String>`. The mutation engine parses strings into `WorkflowStatus` later, so invalid values flow deep into realtime planning and errors are ad-hoc. This keeps workflow invariants out of the type system and duplicates parsing logic.

**Files**
- src/daemon/ops.rs
- src/daemon/mutation_engine.rs
- src/daemon/ipc.rs
design: Change `BeadPatch.status` to a typed patch (`Patch<WorkflowStatus>` or `Patch<Workflow>`). Provide serde mapping at the IPC boundary so JSON still uses `open|in_progress|closed`. Validate/parse in IPC and keep internal code fully typed. Update mutation engine and canonicalization to use the typed status directly; remove string matching.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] `BeadPatch.status` is typed, not `Patch<String>`.
- [ ] Invalid status strings are rejected during IPC parsing with a clear validation error.
- [ ] Mutation engine no longer matches status strings.

description: darin@book
title: darin@book
Allow cycles for Related deps, keep DAG enforcement for Blocks
description:
**Problem**
`apply_add_dep` rejects ANY cycle regardless of dep kind. But "related" edges being acyclic is unnecessarily strict - related beads can absolutely reference each other cyclically.

**Design**
- `Related` and `DiscoveredFrom`: allow cycles (these are informational links)
- `Blocks` and `Parent`: enforce DAG (these have ordering semantics)

**Design Notes**
Open question: what about `DependsOn`? Could argue either way:
- DAG: "cant start A until B done" implies ordering
- Allow cycles: maybe you want mutual dependencies that resolve together?

Suggest: enforce DAG for `DependsOn` initially (safer), can relax later.

**Acceptance**
- [ ] `bd dep add A B --kind=related` succeeds even if B->A exists
- [ ] `bd dep add A B --kind=blocks` still rejects cycles
- [ ] Tests for both cases

**Files:** src/daemon/executor.rs (apply_add_dep), src/core/dep.rs

title: darin@book
tests: realtime fixtures leak daemon processes
description:
**Problem**
Realtime integration tests start the daemon via `bd` auto-start but do not shut it down. `RealtimeFixture` drops only restore env (no `Request::Shutdown`), and `AdminFixture` has no Drop cleanup. `tests/critical_path.rs` and `tests/migration.rs` also auto-start the daemon and never shut it down (only one test does best-effort). This leaves orphaned `bd daemon run` processes, stale sockets/locks, and makes repeated test runs flaky on developer machines and CI.

Evidence:
- `tests/fixtures/realtime.rs` Drop only restores env (lines 77-80).
- `tests/phase7_admin.rs`, `tests/phase7_admin_status.rs`, `tests/phase7_subscribe.rs` use `RealtimeFixture::start_daemon()` and never stop it.
- `tests/critical_path.rs`/`tests/migration.rs` use `bd` CLI without teardown.

**Design**
- Add a shared test helper `shutdown_daemon(runtime_dir: &Path)` (new `tests/fixtures/daemon_runtime.rs` or similar).
- Implement shutdown by sending IPC `Request::Shutdown` to `beads/daemon.sock`, waiting for ack or socket removal; fall back to SIGTERM/SIGKILL if the process is still alive after a timeout.
- Make the helper idempotent (missing socket/meta -> Ok).
- Call this helper from Drop for `RealtimeFixture` and `AdminFixture`, and from the runtime fixture used by `critical_path`/`migration`.

**Acceptance**
- [ ] Any test that auto-starts the daemon shuts it down during Drop (socket + meta removed).
- [ ] `cargo test` leaves no `bd daemon run` processes behind.
- [ ] Helper is used by phase7 realtime tests and other integration tests that spawn the daemon.

**Files:**
- tests/fixtures/realtime.rs
- tests/phase7_admin.rs
- tests/phase7_admin_status.rs
- tests/phase7_subscribe.rs
- tests/critical_path.rs
- tests/migration.rs
- tests/fixtures/daemon_runtime.rs (new)

`git/wire.rs` repeatedly does `String::from_utf8(bytes.to_vec())` for blobs. Avoidable copy.

Use `std::str::from_utf8(bytes)?` and parse from the slice, or stream JSONL via `serde_json::Deserializer`.

CRDT Overhaul Code Review Audit
description:
**Purpose**
Systematic code review of the crdt-overhaul branch (bd-3zoj) organized by logical segments.

**Segments**
1. crdt/1-foundation - OR-Set core types (bd-3zoj.1, bd-3zoj.2)
2. crdt/2-state-model - LabelStore/DepStore/NoteStore + BeadView (bd-3zoj.3, bd-3zoj.4)
3. crdt/3-wire-ops - Wire ops + event validation (bd-3zoj.5, bd-3zoj.6)
4. crdt/4-git-wire - Git wire format (bd-3zoj.10)
5. crdt/5-tests - Roundtrip + determinism tests (bd-3zoj.16, bd-3zoj.17, bd-3zoj.18, bd-3wz4)
6. crdt/6-apply-collision - Deterministic collision handling (bd-3zoj.7)
7. crdt/7-cleanup - Legacy removal (bd-3zoj.11, bd-3zoj.12)

**Process**
Each segment gets a child bead capturing review findings, flagged issues, and resolution status.

title: darin@book
Segment 1: OR-Set Foundation review
description:
**Segment:** crdt/1-foundation (bd-3zoj.1, bd-3zoj.2)
**Files:** src/core/orset.rs, src/core/store_meta.rs, src/daemon/store/runtime.rs
**RepoPrompt chat:** or-set-review-369869

---

## Summary

This segment introduces a foundational **OR-Set (ORSWOT-style)** implementation in `src/core/orset.rs` with:

- `Dot { replica, counter }` and `Dvv { max: Map<ReplicaId, u64> }`
- `OrSet<V>` storing `entries: Map<V, Set<Dot>>` plus a causal context `cc: Dvv`
- `apply_add`, `apply_remove`, and a state-based `join`/`merge`
- deterministic dot-collision resolution ("higher value wins", with a hash fallback)
- unit tests for DVV dominance, `join` commutativity/idempotence, dominance pruning, and collision resolution

Context files indicate the dot-counter plumbing is also in place:
- `StoreMeta` persists `orset_counter` (`#[serde(default)]`)
- `StoreRuntime::next_orset_counter()` increments + writes meta and is covered by a persistence test

## Critical issues (must address)

### 1) **Current `Dvv`/`cc` pruning makes removals delete unrelated values (semantic break)**
The invariant "no dots dominated by `cc` after ops" is enforced via `prune_dominated()`, **but with `Dvv` implemented as only a per-replica max counter, this turns `cc` into an over-approximate tombstone**.

Concretely: any remove that merges a context with `max[r] = c` makes *all dots from replica `r` with counter ≤ c* dominated and therefore pruned — including dots belonging to **other values**.

Minimal failing scenario (this should be a unit test and currently fails with this implementation):

```rust
#[test]
fn remove_does_not_remove_other_values_from_same_replica() {
    let mut set = OrSet::new();
    let d1 = dot(1, 1);
    let d2 = dot(1, 2);

    set.apply_add(d1, "a".to_string(), Sha256([0; 32]));
    set.apply_add(d2, "b".to_string(), Sha256([0; 32]));

    let mut ctx = Dvv::default();
    ctx.observe(d2);
    set.apply_remove(&"b".to_string(), &ctx);

    assert\!(set.contains(&"a".to_string())); // should hold, currently pruned
    assert\!(\!set.contains(&"b".to_string()));
}
```

This is not an edge case — labels/deps will routinely have multiple values added by the same replica with increasing counters, so "remove one label wipes earlier labels from the same author" is catastrophic.

**Design implication:** As written, the CRDT is not an OR-Set for multi-element sets; it behaves more like "per-replica GC-floor" for all earlier dots.

#### What to do
You likely need to change *either* the representation *or* the merge rule:

- **Option A (recommended if you truly mean ORSWOT):** make `cc` an *observed clock* (version vector) and implement the standard ORSWOT merge rule that filters dots using *the other side's clock and presence/absence*, not "drop anything dominated by merged cc". This avoids the "remove b removes a" failure because presence of `a`'s dot on the removing replica prevents it from being treated as removed.
- **Option B (tombstone-style OR-Set):** `cc` must be able to represent **arbitrary sets of removed dots**, not just a per-replica max. That implies a "dot cloud" / exception set (true dotted version vectors) or explicit tombstones. A plain max-per-replica vector cannot represent "removed dot (r,2) but keep dot (r,1) alive".

Right now, `CRDT_OVERHAUL.md` describes `Dvv` exactly as "max per replica" *and* describes pruning by merged cc — those two combined create the bug above. Either the spec needs adjustment, or this code needs to move to the correct ORSWOT rule.

### 2) `OrSet::join` commutative/idempotent tests are insufficient given the above
The provided commutative/idempotent test covers only disjoint replicas/values. Because of the pruning issue, you can still satisfy commutativity/idempotence while being semantically wrong.

Add targeted tests around:
- two values added by the same replica + remove one value
- join across replicas where one side's `cc` is advanced by removing one element but the other element must remain

### 3) "Durable" dot counter: meta writes aren't crash-safe
`StoreRuntime::next_orset_counter()` persists by rewriting JSON via `fs::write`. This is:
- not atomic (crash during write can truncate/corrupt)
- not fsync'd (power loss can roll back the counter)

Given the design intent ("holes OK, reuse not"), **counter rollback risks dot reuse**, which then triggers dot collisions and data loss by deterministic collision winner.

If you need "durable across crashes/power loss", consider:
- write-to-temp + `rename` atomic swap
- directory + file fsync, or store counter in a more crash-resilient store (sqlite / WAL)
- at minimum: atomic replace to avoid truncated JSON

## Minor improvements

- **Layering / dependency hygiene:** `orset.rs` depends on `super::event::Sha256` only for the `_op_hash` argument, but doesn't use it. For a "Layer 3" CRDT primitive, importing from `event` risks future cycles once wire/event code starts depending on OR-Set types. Consider:
  - remove `_op_hash` until needed, or
  - accept `[u8; 32]` directly, or a small hash type defined in a lower layer.
- **Collision hash tie-break is effectively dead code** under correct `Ord/Eq`:
  - dot collision means different values ⇒ `left.cmp(right) \!= Equal`, so hash fallback won't run.
  - If you want the spec's `(value, sha256(dot||value))` literally, it's fine, but acknowledge the hash won't matter unless `Ord` is violated.
- **Missing accessor(s) needed by design:** the spec's remove planning needs the current dots per value. `entries` is private and there's no `dots_for(value)` / `ctx_for(value)` helper yet. That's likely needed soon.

## What's done well

- Clear module-level docs and a readable API surface (`apply_add`, `apply_remove`, `join`, `merge`).
- Determinism-friendly data structures (`BTreeMap`/`BTreeSet`).
- Explicit dot-collision resolution logic exists and is deterministic.
- Store-side dot counter persistence is implemented with a straightforward test showing persistence to disk.

## Actionable next steps

1. **Resolve the core semantic issue:** decide whether this is ORSWOT (recommended) or tombstone OR-Set, then adjust `Dvv` and `OrSet::join` accordingly.
2. Add a **unit test that removing one value does not remove other values from the same replica** (the example above).
3. Add a **join test** that exercises the same scenario across replicas (one side removes one value; the other value must survive join).
4. Decide whether `orset.rs` should depend on `event::Sha256`; remove or refactor `_op_hash` to avoid layer coupling.
5. If "durable counter" is a hard requirement, harden `write_store_meta` to be atomic/fsync-safe to prevent counter rollback and dot reuse.

---

**UPDATE:** Later segments (crdt/4-git-wire+) add `dots_for()` accessor and tests for add-wins semantics (`orset_add_wins_over_remove_ctx`, `orset_join_preserves_concurrent_add`). The critical issue #1 may already be addressed - needs manual verification.

title: darin@book
Segment 2: State Model review
description:
**Segment:** crdt/2-state-model (bd-3zoj.3, bd-3zoj.4)
**Files:** src/core/state.rs, src/core/bead.rs, src/core/dep.rs, +30 others
**RepoPrompt chat:** state-model-review-187BB8

---

## Summary

This patch is test-only: it adds `updated_stamp_includes_labels_and_notes` in `src/core/state.rs` to assert that `CanonicalState::updated_stamp_for()` incorporates:

- the per-bead `LabelState.stamp` (after `apply_label_add`), and
- the latest note's `(at, author)` (after inserting a `Note` into `NoteStore`),

which matches the `CRDT_OVERHAUL.md` updated-stamp rule after labels/notes were moved out of `Bead`.

## Critical issues (invariant risks to address)

### 1) `LabelState.stamp` updates are not monotonic and can regress
In `CanonicalState::{apply_label_add, apply_label_remove}`:

```rs
if !change.is_empty() {
    state.stamp = Some(stamp);
}
```

Problems:
- **No max()**: if an older event is applied later (totally plausible in distributed ingest), `stamp` can move backwards, making `updated_stamp` potentially regress and violating the intent that `updated_stamp` reflect the max across content sources.
- **Change detection is membership-only**: `OrSetChange` is computed from membership sets only; OR-Set *internal* state can change (new dot, cc merge) without changing the visible membership set, and `stamp` won't update. If the stamp is meant to be "last label CRDT activity" (as `CRDT_OVERHAUL.md` suggests: "update store stamp when OR-Set state changes"), this is currently under-counting.

Why this matters to your stated invariants:
- If label operations are applied out of order, `updated_stamp` may no longer be `>=` the true maximum label-related stamp that should participate in resurrection.

**Suggested fix**
- Update `LabelState.stamp` with `max(existing, stamp)` (you already have `set_label_stamp()` that does exactly this; `apply_label_*` should use it).
- If the intended semantic is "OR-Set state changed" (not just membership changed), you'll likely need to extend `OrSet::{apply_add, apply_remove}` (or the returned change type) to indicate **internal mutation** as well as membership diff.

### 2) Legacy dep→OR-Set bridge likely breaks "delete then re-add" for the same dep
In `CanonicalState::insert_dep` (legacy wire path), active membership uses:

```rs
let dot = Self::legacy_dot_for_dep(&merged.created, &key);
self.dep_store.set.apply_add(dot, key.clone(), ...)
```

But `merged.created` is intentionally the **earliest created** stamp (stable provenance), not the "latest active" stamp. With OR-Set semantics, that means:

- Add at `t1` ⇒ dot derived from `t1`
- Delete at `t2` ⇒ remove observes dot(t1) and records it in cc
- Re-add at `t3` (restore) ⇒ **tries dot(t1) again**, but cc dominates it ⇒ add is ignored ⇒ dep never comes back in `dep_store`/`dep_indexes`

This violates the "dep_indexes reflect DepStore membership and behave correctly" expectation in practice, and will make deps non-restorable under the current legacy apply path.

**Suggested fix**
- For "active" membership, derive the dot from the **active-life stamp** (`merged.life.stamp`) rather than `merged.created`, and make the same adjustment in `rebuild_dep_indexes()` (it currently derives from `edge.created`).
- Add a regression test that covers the *real* problematic sequence: **active → deleted → active** (not just deleted→active).

### 3) Current invariant checks don't actually validate `dep_indexes` against `DepStore`
Your proptest `assert_invariants()` checks `dep_indexes` against the legacy `deps` map:

```rs
for (key, edge) in state.deps.iter() { ... }
```

…but `dep_indexes` is explicitly derived from `dep_store` (and rebuilt from `dep_store.values()` after join). If `deps` and `dep_store` ever diverge, this invariant won't catch it.

**Suggested fix**
- Add an invariant check that:
  - every `DepKey` in `dep_store.values()` exists in both indexes, and
  - every edge present in indexes corresponds to a `DepKey` present in `dep_store`.

## Minor improvements

- The new test is good and targeted, but you could make it slightly stronger by asserting full stamp equality after the label add (not just `wall_ms`), e.g. `assert_eq!(updated, label_stamp)`.
- Consider adding tests for:
  - **orphan hiding**: add labels/notes/deps for a missing bead and assert `labels_for/notes_for/deps_from` don't surface them, while the internal stores retain them (`labels_for_any`, `NoteStore`, `dep_store`).
  - **resurrection via labels/notes**: tombstone newer than fields but older than label/note activity should resurrect.
  - **content_hash determinism**: same hash regardless of label/note insertion order and stable across join.
- `compute_content_hash()` appears deterministic, but it likely omits at least `estimated_minutes` (and possibly other fields you intend to be part of OCC). Confirm intent and add a "hash changes when field changes" test if OCC depends on it.

## What's done well

- The added test directly encodes the `CRDT_OVERHAUL.md` updated-stamp rule and will prevent regressions as labels/notes continue moving out of `Bead`.
- The state model separation is clean: `CanonicalState` as the owner of stores + `BeadView` for derived metadata, and deterministic ordering (BTree*, explicit sorts) for stable hashing/output.

## Next steps

1. Fix `LabelState.stamp` maintenance to be monotonic (`max`) and aligned with the intended "OR-Set state changed" semantics.
2. Fix legacy dep dot derivation (`insert_dep` / `rebuild_dep_indexes`) to support delete→re-add.
3. Extend invariant/test coverage to validate `dep_indexes` against **`dep_store`**, and add the missing dep restore regression test.
4. Add deterministic/content-hash regression tests (including field coverage) if `ContentHash` is relied on for OCC.
darin@book
title: darin@book
Segment 3: Wire Ops + Event Validation review
description:
**Segment:** crdt/3-wire-ops (bd-3zoj.5, bd-3zoj.6)
**Files:** src/core/wire_bead.rs, src/core/event.rs, src/daemon/mutation_engine.rs, +10 others
**RepoPrompt chat:** crdt-wire-ops-0FBA1A

---

## Summary (what this segment accomplishes)

This segment completes the "wire ops + validation" portion of the CRDT overhaul by:

- Introducing explicit OR-Set wire operations for labels/deps (`WireLabelAdd/RemoveV1`, `WireDepAdd/RemoveV1`) based on `(dot, ctx/DVV)`.
- Making `TxnDeltaV1` canonical + duplicate-safe via `TxnOpKey` and a `BTreeMap` storage strategy, **including dot in the key for add ops** to prevent dedup collisions.
- Extending CBOR encode/decode in `src/core/event.rs` to support these new ops and the `WireDotV1` / `WireDvvV1` payloads.
- Adding **D1 semantic validation** that rejects workflow/claim patches that would depend on `Keep`.
- Updating mutation planning to mint dots for add ops, build DVV contexts for removes, and to emit workflow/claim patches that always satisfy the new D1 rules—plus validating the assembled `EventBody` before encoding.

Overall, the direction and shape match `CRDT_OVERHAUL.md` and the new invariants are mostly enforced consistently across planning, validation, and apply.

---

## Critical issues (must address)

### 1) `encode_wire_tombstone` can emit invalid CBOR in release builds
**File:** `src/core/event.rs` (`encode_wire_tombstone`)

You compute `has_lineage` as `at.is_some() || by.is_some()` and add `+2` to the encoded map length, but only emit the fields when **both** are present. If only one is set, you hit a `debug_assert\!`—which is compiled out in release—so you will emit a map header declaring too many entries.

That's a correctness bug that can corrupt event bytes / WAL records (and potentially break replication) if a partially-filled tombstone ever reaches encoding.

**Fix suggestion (runtime, not debug-only):**
- Either compute length based on `(Some, Some)` only:
  ```rs
  let has_lineage = tombstone.lineage_created_at.is_some()
      && tombstone.lineage_created_by.is_some();
  if tombstone.lineage_created_at.is_some() ^ tombstone.lineage_created_by.is_some() {
      return Err(EncodeError::InvalidField { ... }); // or similar
  }
  ```
- Or validate the XOR invariant before encoding and error out.

Also consider adding a semantic validation hook for tombstones (same XOR check) since mutation planning now calls `validate_event_body()` but that currently only validates bead patches.

---

### 2) Test fixture creates D1-invalid workflow patches
**File:** `tests/integration/fixtures/event_body.rs` (`sample_bead_patch`)

The fixture sets:
```rs
patch.status = Some(WorkflowStatus::Open);
```
…but does **not** set `closed_reason` / `closed_on_branch`, leaving them at `Keep`.

With the new validator:
```rs
if patch.status.is_some() && (patch.closed_reason.is_keep() || patch.closed_on_branch.is_keep()) { ... }
```
this fixture is now semantically invalid.

Even if current integration tests only call `apply_event()` (and don't validate), anything that goes through `verify_event_frame` / `validate_event_body` (or new mutation-engine validation) will reject these events. This is a likely "tests start failing later" landmine.

**Fix options:**
- Preferred for D1 coverage: keep `status: Some(Open)` but add:
  ```rs
  patch.closed_reason = WirePatch::Clear;
  patch.closed_on_branch = WirePatch::Clear;
  ```
- Or omit `status` entirely in the fixture (leave it `None`) if the intent is "create-like patch".

---

### 3) Potential gap: claim semantic validation allows "unclaimed but expires set"
**File:** `src/core/event.rs` (`validate_bead_patch_semantics`)

Current claim rule only enforces "no Keep asymmetry":
```rs
if assignee_keep ^ expires_keep { error }
```

This still allows **both-not-Keep** combinations like:
- `assignee = Clear`, `assignee_expires = Set(...)` (nonsensical)
- `assignee = Set(...)`, `assignee_expires = Clear` (might be valid if you allow non-expiring claims)

If `build_claim()` rejects some of these, then malformed-but-decodable events can pass `validate_event_body_semantics()` and later fail during apply.

**Action:** confirm intended Claim domain invariants and either:
- tighten validation to reject impossible combos (at minimum: `assignee == Clear` implies `expires == Clear`), or
- ensure `build_claim()` is total and treats any "weird but explicit" combination deterministically (no error).

Given the CRDT goal ("apply should only fail on malformed encodings/invariant violations"), it's better to reject at validation with a clear `EventValidationError::InvalidClaimPatch`.

---

## Minor improvements / quality suggestions

### A) Strengthen workflow validation slightly (optional but safer)
Today you only require "explicit closure fields if status is touched". Two additional checks worth considering:

- Reject `closed_reason` / `closed_on_branch` patches when `status` is `None` (they're ignored by apply anyway; catching them early reduces "silent no-op" footguns).
- Optionally enforce that for `status \!= Closed`, closure patches must be `Clear` (not `Set`) to avoid weird payloads.

### B) Add missing roundtrip + apply coverage for new OR-Set ops
Right now the provided integration tests focus on notes + LWW fields. Add targeted tests for:

- **CBOR** encode/decode roundtrip including:
  - multiple `LabelAdd` for same `(bead_id,label)` with different dots (proves `TxnOpKey` dot inclusion matters)
  - `LabelRemove` and `DepRemove` DVV contexts
- apply idempotency/commutativity for label/dep ops:
  - apply same `LabelAdd` twice (dot idempotency)
  - `Remove(ctx)` then concurrent `Add(dot)` not in ctx (add-wins)
  - `plan_set_parent` producing remove+add converges and doesn't accidentally remove the new parent dot

### C) Docs drift
`CRDT_OVERHAUL.md` still describes:
- `WireDvvV1(Vec<(ReplicaId,u64)>)` but implementation uses a `BTreeMap` and CBOR encodes it as a map.
- `WireDotV1 { replica_id, ... }` vs encoded key `"replica"`.

Recommend updating the doc section so wire format expectations match the actual encoder.

### D) Optional: validate dot origin
Not required by the current spec, but for robustness: consider validating that `LabelAdd.dot.replica` / `DepAdd.dot.replica` matches `EventBody.origin_replica_id`. Otherwise a buggy/malicious replica could mint dots "as" another replica and disrupt DVV monotonic assumptions.

---

## What's done particularly well

- **TxnOpKey includes dot for add ops** (`LabelAdd`, `DepAdd`): this is the correct fix for dedup collisions and aligns with the spec.
- `TxnDeltaV1` using a `BTreeMap<TxnOpKey, TxnOpV1>` provides:
  - canonical op ordering,
  - stable iteration for encoding,
  - and duplicate detection in one place.
- CBOR decode paths are careful about:
  - unique keys (`ensure_unique_key`),
  - per-txn op limits,
  - and note size constraints.
- Mutation engine changes (close/reopen/claim/unclaim/extend_claim + status normalization) are consistent with the D1 validator, and calling `validate_event_body()` before encoding is a strong "fail fast" defense against accidental regression.

---

## Next steps (actionable)

1. **Fix `encode_wire_tombstone`** to enforce lineage field pairing at runtime and to keep map length consistent in release builds.
2. **Update `sample_bead_patch` fixture** to satisfy D1 workflow semantics (explicitly clear closure fields when `status` is set, or omit `status`).
3. Decide on **claim invariants** and either:
   - tighten `validate_bead_patch_semantics` to reject impossible explicit claim combos, or
   - ensure apply is total for all explicit combinations.
4. Add **CBOR + apply tests** covering label/dep OR-set ops (roundtrip, idempotency, add-wins behavior).
5. Update `CRDT_OVERHAUL.md` to reflect the final on-wire encoding choices (DVV map vs vec, dot field/key names).

title: darin@book
Segment 4: Git Wire Format review
description:
**Segment:** crdt/4-git-wire (bd-3zoj.10)
**Files:** src/git/wire.rs, src/git/sync.rs, src/core/state.rs, src/core/orset.rs, src/daemon/migration.rs
**RepoPrompt chat:** git-wire-review-451D06

---

## Summary

This segment keeps the Git wire format deterministic and roundtrippable while adding/using richer CRDT stores:

- `deps.jsonl` supports an OR-Set encoding (`WireDepStore`) and can still parse legacy per-edge JSONL.
- `state.jsonl` embeds per-bead OR-Set label metadata and uses the sparse `_v` mechanism for per-field stamps (SPEC §5.2.1).
- `notes.jsonl` is serialized deterministically and included in `meta.json` checksums (optional on read for legacy commits).
- The actual diff in this slice is a small readability refactor in `parse_deps` (using an `if ... && let Ok(...)` chain) with no intended semantic change.

## Critical issues (must address)

### 1) Integrity hole: `read_state_at_oid` silently ignores malformed `meta.json`
In `src/git/sync.rs`, meta parsing is guarded by an `if let ... && let Ok(meta) = wire::parse_meta(...)` chain. If `meta.json` exists but is corrupted/unparseable, the loader currently:
- drops `checksums` to `None`, and
- proceeds to load state without checksum verification.

This undermines SPEC §5.2 ("Readers MUST verify these checksums when present") and makes corruption harder to detect.

**Action:** Treat an existing-but-unparseable `meta.json` as a hard load failure. Concretely, change the loader logic to:
- if `meta.json` is present → require `parse_meta` succeed (or return error),
- and if parsed checksums exist → always verify.

### 2) Deterministic dep ordering may be spec-ambiguous because `DepKind` ordering is likely enum-order, not lexical
`serialize_deps` sorts entries by `DepKey` (`entries.sort_by(|a,b| a.key.cmp(&b.key))`). `DepKey` derives `Ord`, meaning `(from, to, kind)` ordering uses `DepKind`'s `Ord`.

If `DepKind` is `#[derive(Ord)]` in variant-declaration order (common), that order may not match a lexical `"blocks" < "discovered_from" < "parent" < "related"` interpretation of SPEC §5.3's `(from,to,kind)` ascending requirement.

**Action options:**
- Make `DepKind: Ord` explicitly compare by `as_str()` (recommended for cross-impl determinism).
- Or, keep `DepKind` as-is but **explicitly codify** in SPEC/design docs that ordering uses enum-variant order, not string order.

### 3) MSRV risk: `if let` chains require a minimum Rust version
The diff changes to:

```rust
if non_empty.len() == 1
    && let Ok(wire) = serde_json::from_str::<WireDepStore>(non_empty[0])
{
    ...
}
```

This relies on "let chains" in `if` conditions. Ensure the project's MSRV supports it; otherwise this is a build breaker.

**Action:** Confirm MSRV (Cargo.toml / docs). If MSRV is below the stabilization version, revert to the nested `if` form.

## Minor improvements (quality / robustness)

### A) Normalize OR-Set state on parse (defensive correctness)
Both labels and deps rebuild OR-Set state via `OrSet::from_parts(...)`, which does **not** enforce invariants (e.g., pruning dots dominated by `cc`, resolving dot collisions). That's OK for trusted canonical commits, but it's brittle against:
- corrupted-but-JSON-valid commits (especially if checksums are bypassed as noted above),
- future format changes, or
- manually edited repos.

**Suggestion:** Add a `normalize()` step (or construct via `OrSet::join(&OrSet::new(), &set)`) to ensure:
- dominated dots are pruned,
- collisions are resolved deterministically.

### B) `verify_store_checksums` should not contain an `expect(...)`
```rust
let actual_notes = actual.notes.expect("notes checksum missing");
```

It's safe today because you always compute with `Some(notes_bytes)` in this function, but it's an avoidable panic path.

**Suggestion:** Replace with a normal error if `actual.notes.is_none()` (or compute `actual_notes` directly in the scope where it's needed).

### C) Spec/design drift: `deps.jsonl` OR-Set is a single JSON object line
The design doc slice suggests a JSONL shape with a `cc` line and per-edge lines. Current implementation writes a single `WireDepStore` JSON object with an `entries` array. This is still deterministic, but it:
- makes diffs noisier (any dep change rewrites the whole blob),
- weakens the literal interpretation of SPEC §5.3's "deps.jsonl sorted by (from,to,kind)" (since it's not "one edge per line" anymore).

**Suggestion:** If the intent is to keep JSONL nice for diffs, consider switching `deps.jsonl` OR-Set encoding to multi-line canonical form (cc line + per-key lines), while preserving deterministic order.

## What's done particularly well

- Strong determinism posture: BTreeMap/BTreeSet use throughout, explicit sorting for IDs/keys/dots, and newline normalization for JSONL outputs.
- Backward compatibility: labels can parse legacy arrays, deps can parse legacy edge-per-line JSONL, notes.jsonl is optional on read.
- Roundtrip and property tests cover core determinism and the important "OR-Set metadata persists even when membership is empty" case (add then remove, cc-only state).

## Next steps (actionable)

1. **Make meta parsing strict** in `read_state_at_oid`: fail if `meta.json` exists but can't be parsed; if checksums exist, always verify.
2. **Define dep ordering canonically**:
   - either implement `Ord` for `DepKind` by `as_str()`, or
   - document enum-order as canonical and add a regression test for ordering.
3. **Confirm MSRV** supports `if let` chains; otherwise revert the refactor.
4. (Optional but recommended) **Normalize OR-Set on parse** to prevent accepting invalid OR-Set states.
5. Consider whether `deps.jsonl` should move to **multi-line OR-Set encoding** for better diffs and clearer compliance with SPEC §5.3 ordering language.
darin@book

Segment 5: Tests review
description:
**Segment:** crdt/5-tests (bd-3zoj.16, bd-3zoj.17, bd-3zoj.18, bd-3wz4)
**Files:** src/git/wire.rs, src/core/wire_bead.rs, src/daemon/query_model.rs, src/api/issues.rs, CRDT_OVERHAUL.md
**RepoPrompt chat:** crdt-tests-review-E393FB

---

## Summary (what this segment accomplishes)

This segment adds/expands test coverage around four CRDT-overhaul invariants:

1. **Wire encode/decode roundtrips (realtime + snapshot wire)**  
   `src/core/wire_bead.rs` now has unit tests confirming JSON roundtrips for `WireNoteV1`, `WireBeadPatch` (incl. `WirePatch::Clear`), and `TxnDeltaV1` (incl. `WireDotV1` + `WireDvvV1` through op payloads). It also asserts **canonical op ordering** and **duplicate-key rejection**.

2. **Git/checkpoint roundtrips preserving OR-Set metadata + notes**  
   `src/git/wire.rs` tests now exercise `serialize_*` + `parse_legacy_state` end-to-end and explicitly verify that **OR-Set dots + causal context (DVV)** and **notes** survive a roundtrip.

3. **Deterministic OR-Set serialization**  
   `src/git/wire.rs` includes a "same logical state, different insertion order" test asserting byte-for-byte identical `state.jsonl`, `deps.jsonl`, and `notes.jsonl`.

4. **`updated_at` changes when labels/notes change (no scalar field edit)**  
   `src/core/state.rs` validates `updated_stamp` includes label/note activity, and both `src/api/issues.rs` and `src/daemon/query_model.rs` add tests showing this propagates correctly to `Issue/IssueSummary.updated_at` and `updated_by`.

Overall this aligns well with **CRDT_OVERHAUL.md**'s locked invariants.

---

## Critical issues (should address)

### 1) Determinism test does not vary label/dep stamps, so it may miss a real convergence bug
Your determinism test (`serialize_orset_is_deterministic_across_insertion_order`) varies *operation insertion order*, but all label/dep ops share the same `Stamp` (`stamp.clone()` everywhere). That means it **won't catch** an important class of nondeterminism:

- If `label_stamp` / `dep_store.stamp` are updated by **assignment** (last-applied wins) rather than **`max(existing, incoming)`**, then applying the same set of ops in different orders but with *different stamps* can yield different final metadata → different `updated_stamp` / different serialization bytes.

This matters because **CRDT_OVERHAUL.md's updated_stamp rule** implicitly assumes those stamps are stable CRDT summaries (i.e., merge/order independent).

**Actionable fix (tests):**
- Modify the insertion-order determinism test to use *different* stamps for the two label adds (and separately dep adds), then apply in opposite orders and assert serialization equality.
- Same idea for label add vs label remove: different stamps, reversed order.

If that test fails, it indicates a real CRDT convergence issue in stamp tracking (not just a test issue).

---

## Minor improvements (quality + coverage)

### A) Add a `WireBeadFull` JSON roundtrip test (not just conversion test)
`wire_bead_full_preserves_stamps` validates `BeadView → WireBeadFull → Bead` conversion and `label_stamp()` behavior, but does **not** assert JSON encode/decode roundtrip for `WireBeadFull` itself.

**Suggestion:**
- Add `serde_json::to_string(&wire)` then `from_str` and assert equality.
- Include at least two notes out-of-order to verify the note sorting determinism actually gets exercised (currently only one note).

### B) Add a sparse-`_v` partial coverage test for git wire
In `src/git/wire.rs`, current tests hit:
- `_v` omitted (all fields same as bead stamp), and
- `_v` containing "everything" (when notes force bead-level stamp ahead of all fields).

They do not clearly hit the "**only some fields differ**" case (sparse `_v` correctness).

**Suggestion:**
- Construct a bead where only `title.stamp` is newer than the rest (no notes), serialize/parse/serialize, and assert byte-for-byte equality.
- Add a case where `label_stamp == bead_stamp` so `_v.labels` is omitted and must be reconstructed from default `_at/_by`.

### C) Reduce duplication between API and daemon updated_at tests
`src/api/issues.rs` and `src/daemon/query_model.rs` test modules are essentially identical. That's fine short-term, but it's extra maintenance.

**Suggestion:**
- Factor shared helpers into a small internal test helper module (even just a `#[cfg(test)] mod test_helpers` in `core`).

### D) If legacy parsing is intended to be supported, add explicit legacy-format tests
`parse_deps` and `WireLabels` both support legacy shapes, but this segment's tests primarily validate the new OR-Set format.

**Suggestion:**
- Add a focused unit test that feeds `parse_deps` multiple legacy `WireDep` lines and asserts correct reconstructed state (or explicitly decide legacy isn't required and remove the parser paths).

---

## What's done particularly well

- **End-to-end checksum & roundtrip assertions** in `src/git/wire.rs` are strong; byte-for-byte equality is exactly what you want for the determinism requirement.
- The OR-Set metadata test (`roundtrip_orset_metadata_preserves_dots_and_context`) correctly asserts both:
  - membership dots (`dots_for(...)`), and
  - causal context (`cc()`),
  which directly matches CRDT_OVERHAUL's "persist dots + DVV" invariant.
- The **`updated_at` propagation tests** validate the full pipeline: `CanonicalState.bead_view → BeadView.updated_stamp → Issue/IssueSummary updated_at`, matching the architecture notes.

---

## Next steps (specific, actionable)

1. **Strengthen determinism tests** by using different stamps for label/dep ops in different application orders (this is the biggest missing coverage).
2. Add:
   - `WireBeadFull` JSON roundtrip test (with >1 note to exercise sorting),
   - git wire sparse `_v` partial-diff test.
3. Optional cleanup: dedupe API + daemon updated_at tests via a shared helper module.

Segment 6: Apply Collision Handling review
description:
**Segment:** crdt/6-apply-collision (bd-3zoj.7)
**Files:** src/core/apply.rs, src/core/state.rs, tests/integration/core/apply.rs
**RepoPrompt chat:** crdt-collision-review-B904F2

---

## Summary (what this test change accomplishes)

This patch updates the integration test `note_collision_is_deterministic` to match the **D4 note collision winner rule** by ensuring the colliding note has the **same `(at, author)`** as the existing one, so the test actually exercises the **`sha256(content)` tie-breaker**. It also removes now-unused imports and fixes ownership by cloning `bead_id` when building the `NoteAppend`.

Net effect: the test now correctly validates the "content-hash tiebreak" portion of deterministic note collision handling.

---

## Critical issues / gaps to address

### 1) Integration tests still don't cover most of the collision + totality surface area
Given the scope of "Apply Collision Handling", the integration suite still lacks coverage for:

- **Bead creation collisions**
  - Two `BeadUpsert`s with the same `BeadId` but different `created` stamps
  - Deterministic winner selection + insertion of a **lineage collision tombstone**
  - Order-independence (`apply(A); apply(B)` vs `apply(B); apply(A)`)
  - Suppression of subsequent ops for the losing lineage

- **OR-Set dot collisions (labels + deps)**
  - Same `Dot` used with different values; winner should be deterministic (value lexicographic, then `sha256(dot||value)`)
  - Order-independence of applying the competing adds
  - "stamp updates only on real membership change" behavior around collisions

- **Orphan totality rules**
  - `LabelAdd/Remove`, `DepAdd/Remove`, and `NoteAppend` on **missing beads** must not error
  - Orphans should become visible after later bead creation (queries hide until bead exists)

Right now, the integration file only covers: idempotency, one note collision tiebreak, and LWW ordering.

### 2) Merge-path determinism remains a risk (not tested here)
Even though this diff only touches tests, it's worth calling out: the merge side must align with apply for D4 to be "done".

- `CanonicalState::join` currently **keeps the left bead** on `Bead::join` collision while collecting errors (non-commutative in the presence of collisions).
- `NoteStore::join` is left-biased for same `(bead_id, note_id)`.

If collisions can exist in inputs to `join()` (e.g., from older snapshots/checkpoints, manual state construction, or partial replication paths), this violates the "deterministic everywhere" goal. There are no integration tests that would catch this divergence.

---

## Minor improvements (quality / robustness)

- In `note_collision_is_deterministic`, consider asserting the intended preconditions explicitly to prevent fixture drift from accidentally changing what the test exercises:
  - `assert_eq\!(stored.at, expected_at)` isn't possible via `notes_for()` refs unless you also pull `note.at` and `note.author` from both versions, but you can at least assert the incoming `note.at/author` match the base fixture's values before applying.

- The expected-winner computation could mirror the spec tuple ordering even though this test currently pins `(at, author)` equal. That would make the test self-documenting and resilient if `sample_note()` changes.

---

## What's done particularly well

- **Intentionality:** Switching from a hand-constructed `WireNoteV1` (with different `at/author`) to `sample_note(2, 1)` makes it clear the test is about the **hash tie-break**, not timestamp/author precedence.
- **Cleanliness:** Removing `WireNoteV1`/`WireStamp` imports and fixing the `bead_id` move with `bead_id.clone()` keeps the test correct and idiomatic.

---

## Actionable next steps

1. **Add integration tests for bead ID collisions**:
   - Apply two conflicting creations in both orders; assert same winner + lineage tombstone behavior.
2. **Add integration tests for OR-Set dot collisions** (labels + deps):
   - Same `Dot`, different value; ensure deterministic resolution + order-independence.
3. **Add integration tests for orphan ops**:
   - Apply label/dep/note ops before bead exists; ensure no error and visibility after bead creation.
4. **Add tests around "stamp updates only on real membership change"**:
   - Add a second dot for the same label/dep value (membership unchanged) and assert stamp/`updated_stamp` doesn't advance.
5. (If D4 is meant to include merge right now) **Add merge/join collision tests** or update merge to apply the same collision comparators as apply.

Segment 7: Cleanup review
description:
**Segment:** crdt/7-cleanup (bd-3zoj.11, bd-3zoj.12)
**Files:** 33 files, -880 lines net (major deletions: src/git/collision.rs, DepEdge/DepLife, NoteLog)
**RepoPrompt chat:** crdt-cleanup-review-801B27

---

## Summary (what these changes accomplish)

- **WAL snapshot dep serialization is now JSON-safe and deterministic**: `DepStore` is encoded as a `WalDepStore { cc, entries[], stamp }` (mirrors the checkpoint/git approach of "entry lists"), and legacy dep snapshots are still convertible via `dep_store_from_legacy`.
- **Core CRDT model looks cleaned of legacy dep/note/label structures** in the provided context:
  - Core deps are `DepKey` OR-Set membership (no core `DepEdge`/`DepLife`).
  - Notes are in `NoteStore` (no `NoteLog` embedded in `Bead`).
  - Labels are OR-Set state in `LabelStore` (no LWW label field in `BeadFields`).
- **Git sync merge path is simplified**: `src/git/sync.rs` merges via `CanonicalState::join` directly; there's no collision "remap" module or call site shown.

## Critical issues / must-address

### 1) "Collision ApplyError paths are gone" is **not true yet**
In `src/core/apply.rs` the enum still includes:
- `ApplyError::BeadCollision`
- `ApplyError::NoteCollision`

And at least one is still referenced:

```rs
state
  .insert(bead)
  .map_err(|_| ApplyError::BeadCollision { id: id.clone() })?;
```

Even if this is "effectively unreachable" after the earlier collision-handling logic, it still violates the segment 7 acceptance criterion you cited ("ApplyError/collision legacy paths are gone").

**Action**:
- If collisions are now always resolved deterministically, **remove these variants** and refactor the remaining callsites to be infallible (or treat failures as internal invariants with `expect`/panic + metrics).
- If there are still legitimate failure cases, document them explicitly and reconcile with the "apply total" constraint.

### 2) WAL format change may break "recent-but-not-ancient" WAL snapshots
`WalStateV2.deps` changed from `DepStore` → `WalDepStore`. With the current untagged enum:

```rs
enum WalStateRepr {
  V2(WalStateV2),        // now expects WalDepStore
  LegacyVecs(...),       // expects Vec<LegacyWalDep>
  LegacyMaps(...),       // expects BTreeMap<DepKey, ...>
}
```

A WAL written by the immediately previous code (when deps were a `DepStore` JSON object) likely won't match *any* variant, because:
- it won't deserialize as `WalDepStore`, and
- it won't look like the legacy vec/map shapes.

Given your global constraint ("old local stores unsupported; wipe"), this might be acceptable — but then keeping legacy parsing paths is a bit inconsistent.

**Action** (pick one):
- **Accept breakage** and explicitly document that WAL files from earlier builds are unsupported and should be deleted on upgrade.
- Or add a transitional variant that can deserialize the previous `DepStore`-shaped deps (even if only for dev ergonomics).

### 3) `WalDepStore::into_dep_store` inserts empty-dot entries (potentially unsafe)
This code:

```rs
if dots.is_empty() {
    map.entry(entry.key).or_default();
} else {
    map.insert(entry.key, dots);
}
```

Risks creating OR-Set `entries` containing keys with empty dot sets. Whether that's harmless depends on `OrSet` invariants:
- If `OrSet::values()`/`is_empty()`/`contains()` assume "no empty dot sets", you can get subtle bugs (phantom values, incorrect emptiness, etc.).

**Action**:
- Prefer **skipping empty-dot entries entirely**, or normalize through an `OrSet` constructor that prunes empties.
- If empty entries are intentionally meaningful, add an explicit comment explaining why and ensure `OrSet` APIs treat them correctly.

## Minor improvements (quality/cleanup)

- **DRY**: `WalDepStore` is structurally very similar to `WireDepStoreV1`/`WireDepEntryV1`. If layering allows, consider reusing the existing wire structs (or extracting a shared internal representation) to avoid divergence.
- **`Labels::remove` returns `true` unconditionally** (`src/core/collections.rs`). That's misleading and can hide bugs at call sites. Either:
  - return the real "changed?" boolean (`before_len \!= after_len`), or
  - make it `fn remove(&mut self, label: &str)` without a return value.

## What's done particularly well

- **The WAL deps change is targeted and solves a real problem**: serializing `BTreeMap<DepKey, ...>` to JSON is not viable (non-string keys), and the new entry-list format aligns with the checkpoint/git dep encoding.
- **Git sync is now cleanly CRDT-based**: merge uses `CanonicalState::join` with no sign of ID remap collision hacks, which is exactly the desired direction.
- **Core model cleanup appears consistent**: DepKey-only deps, OR-Set labels, NoteStore notes, and no bead-embedded NoteLog in the shown files.

## Specific next steps

1. **Remove collision error variants** (`ApplyError::BeadCollision`, `ApplyError::NoteCollision`) and any remaining call sites that can surface them, or justify them with explicit invariants/docs.
2. **Decide on WAL backward-compat stance**:
   - If "wipe is fine", add a comment and possibly a more explicit error message instructing deletion.
   - If not, add a transitional deserialization branch for the previous `DepStore` JSON shape.
3. **Prune empty-dot handling** in `WalDepStore::into_dep_store` unless you can prove `OrSet` tolerates it.
4. Run a final grep-based audit (or CI check) for the explicit delete list tokens:
   - `NoteLog`, `DepLife`, core `DepEdge`, `WireDepV1`, `WireDepDeleteV1`, `WireBeadPatch.labels`, `git::collision`/remap callsites, and the collision ApplyError variants.
darin@book
title: darin@book
Add --parent flag to bd update command
description:
**Problem**
Cannot reparent a bead after creation. Beads created independently cannot be linked to an epic as children later.

Currently `--parent` only works at create time:
```
bd create "subtask" --parent=bd-epic
```

**Solution**
Add `--parent` flag to `bd update`:
```
bd update bd-xyz --parent=bd-epic
```

**Design Notes**
- Should validate parent exists and is not deleted
- Should prevent circular parent relationships
- Consider `--parent=""` or `--no-parent` to unparent a bead
- Parent changes should be LWW like other fields
- ID format: beads created with --parent get hierarchical IDs (bd-abc.1). Reparenting existing beads probably keeps their original ID rather than renumbering.

**Acceptance**
- [ ] `bd update <id> --parent <epic-id>` works
- [ ] Invalid/deleted parent rejected with clear error
- [ ] Circular parent reference rejected
- [ ] Can unparent a bead (remove from epic)
- [ ] `bd show` reflects new parent
- [ ] `bd epic status` includes reparented beads
