title: Add Slot issue type support
description:
**Problem**
beads-rs lacks the Slot issue type - exclusive access primitives that coordinate shared resources with a holder and waiters queue. Slots enforce mutual exclusion for resources like merge queues, deployment slots, or critical sections.

**What Slot Beads Do (from Go beads)**
Slot beads manage exclusive access:
- One holder at a time (mutual exclusion)
- Waiters queue for access
- Automatic slot release when holder closes/releases
- Used for: merge queues, deployment slots, exclusive workflows

Slot lifecycle:
1. Create slot (empty, no holder)
2. Agent claims slot (becomes holder)
3. Agent uses resource
4. Agent releases slot (holder becomes empty)
5. Next waiter (if any) is notified

Merge-slot gate pattern:
- Refinery uses merge-slot to serialize merges
- Only one PR can merge at a time
- Other PRs wait in queue

**Go Implementation**
Type definition (internal/types/types.go):
- IssueType = TypeSlot (line 495)
- Fields (lines 99-100):
  - Holder string: Who currently holds the slot (empty = available)
  - Waiters []string: Queue of waiting agents

Slot operations (cmd/bd/slot.go for agent slots, cmd/bd/merge_slot.go for merge slots):
- Claim slot: Set holder if empty, else add to waiters
- Release slot: Clear holder, notify first waiter
- Check slot: Query holder and waiters

Agent slots (cmd/bd/slot.go):
Different concept - slots ON agent beads (hook, role)
These are fields, not separate slot issue types.

Merge-slot example (cmd/bd/merge_slot.go):
- Create merge-slot bead for rig
- Refinery claims slot before merge
- Other refineries wait
- Release on merge complete

**Design for Rust**
Data model (src/core/):
```rust
pub struct SlotData {
    holder: Option<String>,  // Current holder (empty = available)
    waiters: Vec<String>,    // Queue of waiting agents
}
```

Add to Bead or use optional fields.

Slot operations (src/daemon/ or src/api/):
```rust
pub fn claim_slot(slot_id: &str, claimant: &str) -> Result<ClaimResult, Error>;
pub fn release_slot(slot_id: &str, holder: &str) -> Result<(), Error>;
pub fn slot_status(slot_id: &str) -> Result<SlotStatus, Error>;

pub enum ClaimResult {
    Claimed,       // You are now the holder
    Queued,        // Added to waiters
    AlreadyHolder, // You already hold it
}

pub struct SlotStatus {
    holder: Option<String>,
    waiters: Vec<String>,
    position: Option<usize>, // Your position if in waiters
}
```

CLI (src/cli/):
- bd slot-claim <slot-id> [<agent>] - Claim or join queue
- bd slot-release <slot-id> [<agent>] - Release and notify next
- bd slot-status <slot-id> - Show holder and queue
- bd create --type slot "Merge queue"

Notification:
- When slot released, notify first waiter
- Integration with message system or external notification

**Design Notes**
- Slots vs agent slots: Different concepts!
  - Slot issue type: Exclusive resource coordination
  - Agent slot fields: Work/role references on agents
- Fairness: FIFO queue for waiters
- Consider: Slot timeout (auto-release if holder doesn't heartbeat)
- Consider: Slot priority (high-priority claims jump queue)
- Consider: Multi-slot (lease N slots from pool)
- Consider: Deadlock detection (slot dependency cycles)

**Acceptance**
- [ ] Slot type constant defined
- [ ] SlotData struct with holder, waiters
- [ ] claim_slot operation implemented
- [ ] release_slot operation implemented
- [ ] slot_status query implemented
- [ ] bd slot-claim works
- [ ] bd slot-release works
- [ ] bd slot-status shows holder and queue
- [ ] bd create --type slot works
- [ ] Claim enforces mutual exclusion
- [ ] Release notifies next waiter
- [ ] Tests for slot claim/release
- [ ] Tests for waiter queue ordering
- [ ] Migration from Go beads preserves slot type and fields

**Files to study in Go beads:**
- tmp/beads/internal/types/types.go (lines 99-100: holder field, line 495: TypeSlot)
- tmp/beads/cmd/bd/merge_slot.go (merge-slot coordination example)
- tmp/beads/cmd/bd/slot.go (agent slot management - different concept)

title: llms.txt standard - AI agent discoverability
description:
**Problem**
AI agents need a standard way to discover project-specific instructions, APIs, and tools. The llms.txt standard (https://llmstxt.org/) provides a well-known endpoint for agent discoverability. Beads should generate this automatically based on project state.

**What the Feature Does**
Automatically generate `/llms.txt` (or `.beads/llms.txt`) containing:
- Project overview from README
- Available beads commands (bd create, bd ready, etc.)
- Current issues and status
- Project-specific workflows
- Agent instructions from AGENTS.md
- Integration points (git hooks, sync, etc.)

**Example Output**:
```
# My Project (bd)

> Git-backed issue tracker for AI workflows.

## Essential Commands

bd create "Title" -p 1 --json      # Create issue
bd ready --json                     # Show ready work
bd sync                             # Sync to git

## Current Work (Auto-generated)

Open Issues: 12
- bd-abc (P0): Critical bug in sync
- bd-xyz (P1): Implement feature X

Ready to Work: 5 issues
Blocked: 3 issues

## Agent Workflows

See AGENTS.md for complete guide.
Always use --json for programmatic access.
Run `bd sync` at end of session.

## Documentation

- Full docs: ./docs/
- API: Run `bd help --json`
```

**Why It Needs Design Work**

1. **Content Strategy**:
   - What to include? Too much = noise, too little = not useful
   - Static content (commands, docs) vs dynamic (current issues)
   - Freshness - regenerate when? After every operation?
   - Caching strategy?

2. **Generation Model**:
   - Option A: Generate on-demand (`bd llms-txt`)
   - Option B: Auto-generate after mutations (create, update, close)
   - Option C: Web server endpoint (daemon serves it)
   - Option D: Static file committed to repo
   - Which fits beads workflow best?

3. **Content Depth**:
   - Minimal: Just commands and project name
   - Medium: Commands + current work summary  
   - Full: Entire state dump (issues, deps, labels)
   - How to balance discoverability vs overwhelming?

4. **Multi-Repo Handling**:
   - Single repo: Easy, one llms.txt
   - Multi-repo/orchestrator: Which issues to show?
   - Aggregate all rigs?
   - Per-rig llms.txt?

**Key Decisions to Make**

1. **File Location**:
   - Standard: `/llms.txt` in repo root (web-accessible)
   - Beads-specific: `.beads/llms.txt`
   - Both?
   - User-configurable via config?

2. **Generation Trigger**:
   ```
   Option A: Manual
   - bd llms-txt > /llms.txt
   - User commits to repo
   
   Option B: Auto-update
   - After bd create/update/close
   - Daemon background task
   
   Option C: Dynamic
   - Daemon serves endpoint
   - Not a file, just API
   
   Option D: Hybrid
   - bd sync generates static file
   - Always fresh after sync
   ```

3. **Content Template**:
   ```markdown
   # {project_name}
   
   > {description from README or config}
   
   ## Quick Start
   {from README or QUICKSTART.md}
   
   ## Essential Commands
   {list of bd commands with examples}
   
   ## Current State (as of {timestamp})
   - Open: {count}
   - Ready: {count}
   - Blocked: {count}
   
   Top Priority:
   {top 5 ready issues}
   
   ## Workflows
   {from AGENTS.md if exists}
   
   ## Documentation
   {links to docs, README, etc}
   ```

4. **Dynamic Content Freshness**:
   - Include timestamp?
   - Show staleness warning if old?
   - Auto-regenerate on `bd sync`?
   - Manual refresh command?

**Trade-offs to Consider**

1. **Static vs Dynamic**:
   - Static: Works with any web server, git-tracked
   - Dynamic: Always fresh, but requires daemon
   - Hybrid: Best of both?

2. **Detail Level**:
   - Detailed: More useful for agents
   - Minimal: Cleaner, less maintenance
   - Solution: Configurable verbosity?

3. **Performance**:
   - Full state dump = expensive
   - Summary = fast but less useful
   - Cache + incremental updates?

4. **Git Pollution**:
   - Auto-generated files in commits = noisy
   - Gitignore llms.txt and serve dynamically?
   - Or embrace it (it's useful)?

**Reference Files in Go Beads**
- `/Users/darin/Projects/beads-rs-macstudio/tmp/beads/website/static/llms.txt` - Example output
- Search for "llms" in codebase

**Go Implementation** (if exists):
Currently Go beads has a static `website/static/llms.txt` manually maintained.
No auto-generation found in codebase - opportunity to pioneer!

**llms.txt Standard**
From https://llmstxt.org/:
- Markdown format
- Well-known location: `/llms.txt`
- Designed for language models to read
- Should be concise but comprehensive
- Include project context, APIs, current state
- Links to fuller documentation

**Use Cases to Support**

1. **Agent Onboarding**:
   - New AI agent joins project
   - Reads llms.txt to understand project
   - Gets current work, commands, workflows

2. **Context Refresh**:
   - Long-running session
   - Agent re-reads llms.txt for latest state
   - Discovers new issues, blockers

3. **Cross-Project Discovery**:
   - Agent working on multiple projects
   - Quickly switch context via llms.txt
   - Consistent format across projects

4. **Documentation Portal**:
   - Web-accessible project overview
   - Human-readable too
   - Living documentation

**Content Sections to Consider**

1. **Project Metadata**:
   - Name, description
   - Repository URL
   - License
   - Key maintainers

2. **Quick Start**:
   - Installation
   - Basic commands
   - First steps

3. **Current State**:
   - Issue counts by status
   - Top priority work
   - Recent activity

4. **Commands**:
   - Essential bd commands
   - JSON output examples
   - Common patterns

5. **Workflows**:
   - Agent-specific instructions
   - Session protocol
   - Best practices

6. **Documentation Links**:
   - README
   - AGENTS.md
   - API docs
   - Specs

**Open Design Questions**

1. Should llms.txt be versioned?
   - Include API version, schema version?
   - Help agents understand format changes?

2. Multiple formats?
   - llms.txt (markdown)
   - llms.json (structured data)
   - llms.html (pretty view)

3. Privacy/security?
   - Filter sensitive issues?
   - Public vs private sections?
   - Authentication for full details?

4. Customization?
   - Template system?
   - User-provided sections?
   - Plugin hooks to add content?

5. Standard vs beads-specific?
   - Follow llms.txt spec strictly?
   - Or extend with beads-specific sections?
   - Namespace extensions?

**Integration Points**

- `bd info --json` - Current state data
- `bd ready --json` - Top priority work
- `bd list --json` - All issues
- `bd help` - Command reference
- README.md - Project description
- AGENTS.md - Agent workflows
- CLAUDE.md - Agent instructions

**Acceptance**
- [ ] Design document for llms.txt generation
- [ ] Content template and structure
- [ ] Generation trigger strategy (manual, auto, dynamic)
- [ ] File location(s) decision
- [ ] Dynamic content freshness approach
- [ ] Multi-repo handling
- [ ] Privacy/filtering rules
- [ ] Performance considerations
- [ ] Integration with bd sync workflow
- [ ] Template customization mechanism
- [ ] Example output for beads-rs itself

title: Add custom types system support
description:
**Problem**
beads-rs has hardcoded issue types (bug, feature, task, epic, chore). Users cannot define project-specific types (experiment, spike, incident, etc.). This limits expressiveness and forces users to shoehorn domain concepts into generic types.

**What Custom Types Enable (from Go beads)**
Custom types allow projects to define their own vocabulary:
- Research projects: experiment, hypothesis, analysis
- SRE: incident, postmortem, oncall
- Design: mockup, prototype, usability-test
- Any domain-specific concept

Configuration:
```toml
[types]
custom = ["experiment", "spike", "incident", "postmortem"]
```

Validation:
- Built-in types: Always valid (catch typos)
- Custom types: Valid per project (trust local config)
- Federation: Trust child repo's custom types (don't re-validate)

**Go Implementation**
Type validation (internal/types/types.go):
```go
// IsValid checks built-in types only
func (t IssueType) IsValid() bool {
    switch t { case TypeBug, TypeFeature, ... }
}

// IsValidWithCustom checks built-in + custom
func (t IssueType) IsValidWithCustom(customTypes []string) bool {
    if t.IsValid() { return true }
    for _, custom := range customTypes {
        if string(t) == custom { return true }
    }
    return false
}
```

Config integration (internal/configfile/config.go):
```go
type Config struct {
    Types struct {
        Custom []string `toml:"custom"`
    } `toml:"types"`
}
```

CLI usage:
- bd config set types.custom "exp,spike,incident"
- bd create --type experiment "Test hypothesis"
- bd list --type experiment

Federation trust model (lines 367-391):
When importing from child repo:
- Built-in types: Validate (catch typos like "tsak")
- Custom types: Trust (child already validated)
This implements "trust the chain below you" from HOP.

**Design for Rust**
Data model (src/core/):
```rust
pub enum IssueTypeVariant {
    Bug, Feature, Task, Epic, Chore,
    Gate, Agent, Role, Rig, Convoy, Event, Slot,
    Message, MergeRequest, Molecule,
    Custom(String),  // Project-specific types
}

impl IssueTypeVariant {
    pub fn is_builtin(&self) -> bool;
    pub fn validate(&self, custom_types: &[String]) -> bool;
}
```

Config (src/config/):
```toml
[types]
custom = ["experiment", "spike"]
```

Parse in Config struct:
```rust
pub struct Config {
    pub types: TypesConfig,
}

pub struct TypesConfig {
    pub custom: Vec<String>,
}
```

Validation:
```rust
impl Bead {
    pub fn validate(&self, config: &Config) -> Result<(), Error> {
        self.issue_type.validate(&config.types.custom)?;
        // ...
    }
}
```

CLI (src/cli/):
- bd config set types.custom "exp,spike"
- bd config get types.custom
- bd create --type <custom-type>
- bd list --type <custom-type>

**Design Notes**
- Custom types are strings, not validated at compile time
- Type aliases (feat → feature) still useful for custom types
- Federation: Import trusts source repo's custom types
- Consider: Type hierarchy/inheritance (spike extends experiment)
- Consider: Type-specific field requirements (validate by type)
- Consider: Type namespacing (rig:experiment vs hq:experiment)
- Consider: Type discovery (bd types list --all shows built-in + custom)

**Acceptance**
- [ ] IssueTypeVariant enum with Custom(String) variant
- [ ] Config.types.custom field
- [ ] bd config set/get types.custom works
- [ ] Validation allows built-in + custom types
- [ ] Validation rejects unknown types
- [ ] bd create --type <custom> works
- [ ] bd list --type <custom> filters correctly
- [ ] bd types command shows built-in + custom
- [ ] Federation import trusts custom types from source
- [ ] Tests for custom type validation
- [ ] Tests for config persistence
- [ ] Migration from Go beads preserves custom types in config

**Files to study in Go beads:**
- tmp/beads/internal/types/types.go (lines 518-529: IsValidWithCustom)
- tmp/beads/internal/types/types.go (lines 367-391: federation trust model)
- tmp/beads/internal/configfile/config.go (custom types config)
- tmp/beads/cmd/bd/create.go (type validation with custom)

title: Add formula system to beads-rs
description:
**Problem**
beads-rs needs the formula system - declarative workflow templates that compile to protos. Formulas are the source layer above protos, enabling composition, inheritance, and complex workflow patterns.

**What are Formulas?**
Formulas are .formula.json/.formula.toml files that define workflows declaratively:
- Variable definitions with defaults, validation, enums
- Steps with dependencies (depends_on, needs, waits_for)
- Composition via extends, aspects, bond points
- Control flow: loops, branches, gates

**How it Works (Go beads)**
1. Parser loads formula from search paths (.beads/formulas/, ~/.beads/formulas/)
2. Resolve inheritance (extends field)
3. Apply transformations: control flow, aspects, inline expansion
4. Cook to proto (ephemeral subgraph in memory) OR persist to DB
5. Pour/wisp instantiates the proto with variable substitution

**Search Paths**
1. .beads/formulas/ (project)
2. ~/.beads/formulas/ (user)  
3. $GT_ROOT/.beads/formulas/ (orchestrator)

**Commands**
- bd formula list - list available formulas
- bd formula show <name> - show formula details
- bd cook <formula> - compile formula to proto (ephemeral by default)
- bd cook <formula> --persist - save proto to DB
- bd pour <formula> --var k=v - inline cook + instantiate persistent
- bd mol wisp <formula> --var k=v - inline cook + instantiate ephemeral

**Design Considerations**
- Parser in Rust using serde for JSON/TOML
- Formula struct mirrors Go internal/formula/formula.go
- Variable substitution using {{var}} syntax
- Inheritance resolver (extends field)
- Aspect system (AOP-style advice: before, after, around)
- Control flow operators (loops, branches, gates)

**Files to Study (Go beads)**
- cmd/bd/cook.go - formula cooking logic
- cmd/bd/formula.go - formula commands (list, show)
- internal/formula/ - parser, resolver, transformations
- .beads/formulas/beads-release.formula.toml - complex example

**Acceptance**
- [ ] Formula parser for JSON/TOML
- [ ] Variable substitution engine
- [ ] Inheritance resolver (extends)
- [ ] bd formula list/show commands
- [ ] bd cook compiles formula to proto
- [ ] Integration with pour/wisp
- [ ] Tests for formula compilation

title: Implement bd orphans command - Find issues referenced in commits but never closed
design:
Port the orphan detection feature from Go beads to beads-rs.

## What it does

Scans git commit history for issue IDs (bd-xxx pattern) and identifies issues that:
1. Are referenced in commit messages
2. Remain in open or in_progress status in the database
3. Should have been closed but weren't

This helps find work that's been implemented but not formally closed.

## Implementation approach

**Location**: `src/cli/orphans.rs` (new file), exposed via `src/cli/mod.rs`

**Command structure**:
```rust
pub struct OrphansArgs {
    #[arg(long)]
    json: bool,
    #[arg(long)]
    details: bool,
    #[arg(long, short)]
    fix: bool,  // Close orphaned issues with confirmation
}
```

**Core logic**:
1. Use git2-rs to walk commit history
2. Scan commit messages for bd-xxx patterns using regex
3. Query daemon for status of each referenced issue
4. Filter for issues still open/in_progress
5. Build orphan list with metadata (latest commit hash, message)

**Output**:
- Human mode: Pretty table with issue ID, title, status, latest commit
- JSON mode: Machine-readable array of orphan objects
- Details mode: Include full commit info

**Fix mode**:
- Confirm with user (Y/n prompt)
- Call daemon to close each orphaned issue with reason "Implemented"
- Report success/failure for each

## Files to reference

Go implementation: `/Users/darin/Projects/beads-rs-macstudio/tmp/beads/cmd/bd/orphans.go`
Uses shared doctor package: `/Users/darin/Projects/beads-rs-macstudio/tmp/beads/cmd/bd/doctor/`

## Design notes

- **Shared utility**: The orphan detection logic should be in `src/core/` or `src/git/` as a reusable function, not just in CLI.
- **Pattern matching**: Use same regex pattern as Go beads for consistency: `bd-[a-z0-9]{5,6}`
- **Performance**: For large repos, consider limiting commit scan depth or adding --since flag
- **Edge cases**: 
  - External references (external:xxx) should be ignored
  - Closed issues should be filtered out
  - Tombstones (deleted issues) should be handled

## Questions to resolve

1. Should orphan detection be part of `bd doctor` or standalone command?
2. Do we want to scan all branches or just current branch?
3. Should we cache commit scan results?

title: Add duplicate command for deduplication links
description:
**Problem**
Go beads supports `bd duplicate <id> --of <canonical>` to mark an issue as a duplicate and automatically close it. This is essential for large databases with many similar reports. beads-rs needs this.

**How it works in Go beads**
- Command: `bd duplicate <id> --of <canonical>` (in cmd/bd/duplicate.go)
- Uses `DepDuplicates` dependency type
- Automatically closes the duplicate issue
- Close reason references the canonical issue
- Stored in dependencies table (per Decision 004: Edge Schema Consolidation)

**Design for Rust**
1. Add `Duplicates` variant to `DepKind` enum in src/core/domain.rs
2. Mark it as non-DAG-enforcing (informational link)
3. Add CLI command: src/cli/commands/duplicate.rs
   - `bd duplicate <id> --of <canonical>`
   - Validates canonical issue exists
   - Rejects self-duplication (id == canonical)
   - Creates duplicates edge
   - Closes duplicate issue atomically
4. Wire through daemon IPC for atomic operation (add dep + close)
5. Add to CLI command tree

**Design considerations**
- Atomicity: dependency + closure must be atomic
- Should we allow closing already-closed duplicates? (Go allows it)
- Close reason should reference canonical ID
- Prevent duplicate-of-duplicate chains? (Go doesn't enforce this)
- What if canonical is itself a duplicate? (follow chain to root?)

**Design questions for human review**
- Should duplicate edges be unidirectional or bidirectional?
  * Go: unidirectional (duplicate -> canonical)
  * Benefit: canonical can list "all duplicates of me"
- Should we auto-cascade when canonical is closed?
  * Or keep duplicates independent?
- Display strategy: show "duplicate of X" in bd show/list?

**Files to study**
- tmp/beads/cmd/bd/duplicate.go - Go implementation
- tmp/beads/internal/types/types.go - DepDuplicates type
- src/core/bead.rs - bead workflow and closure semantics

**Acceptance**
- [ ] Duplicates added to DepKind enum
- [ ] bd duplicate command creates edge and closes issue
- [ ] Self-duplication rejected
- [ ] Canonical issue validated to exist
- [ ] JSON output format matches Go beads
- [ ] Cargo fmt, clippy, test pass

title: Tip system with contextual hints
description:
**Problem**
Users need contextual guidance after commands. Go beads has a tip system that shows hints based on conditions, with frequency throttling and probability rolls.

**Design**
Implement tip system with:
- `Tip` struct: ID, condition function, message, frequency (min gap), priority, probability (0.0-1.0)
- `maybeShowTip()`: selects and displays tip respecting --json and --quiet flags
- `selectNextTip()`: filters eligible tips by condition + frequency, sorts by priority, applies probability roll
- `BEADS_TIP_SEED` env var for deterministic testing
- Metadata storage: `tip_{id}_last_shown` timestamp in database

Selection algorithm:
1. Filter to eligible tips (condition true AND frequency elapsed)
2. Sort by priority (descending)
3. Apply probability roll in priority order (higher priority tips get first chance)

Built-in tips:
- Claude setup: priority 100, 24h gap, 60% probability when Claude detected but not configured
- Sync conflict: priority 200, no frequency limit, 100% probability (always show when sync broken)

Reference: `/Users/darin/Projects/beads-rs-macstudio/tmp/beads/cmd/bd/tips.go`

**Design Notes**
- Tips are informational, not errors (use stdout, not stderr)
- Skip in JSON mode or quiet mode
- Thread-safe tip registry with RwLock
- InjectTip/RemoveTip for dynamic tips at runtime
- Use chrono for time parsing/formatting (RFC3339)

**Acceptance**
- [ ] Tip struct with condition, message, frequency, priority, probability
- [ ] maybeShowTip respects --json and --quiet
- [ ] selectNextTip filters by condition and frequency
- [ ] Priority-ordered probability rolls (higher priority first)
- [ ] BEADS_TIP_SEED for deterministic testing
- [ ] Built-in Claude setup and sync conflict tips
- [ ] Metadata tracking (last shown timestamp)

**Files:**
- `src/cli/tips.rs` (new)
- `src/cli/mod.rs` (integrate into post-command hook)

description: darin@book
title: darin@book
mutation: required fields must be non-empty on update
description:
Problem
- ParsedBeadPatch::parse only blocks Patch::Clear for title/description but allows Patch::Set("") or whitespace.
- Creates invalid state: required fields present but empty.
- Create path enforces non-empty; update does not.

Impact
- Invariant drift between create/update; data quality issues and UI regressions.
- Not encoded in types (string vs non-empty string).
design:
Design
- Introduce NonEmptyString (or reuse existing type) for required fields at the parse boundary.
- Normalize (trim) and reject empty values during ParsedBeadPatch::parse.
- Make CanonicalBeadPatch carry validated non-empty strings for title/description patches, so downstream code never sees invalid values.

Scatter fit
- Single validation point in parse; no downstream checks.

Files
- crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/mutation_engine.rs
- crates/beads-surface/src/ops.rs (if adding NonEmptyString)
- crates/beads-core/src/event.rs (if validating patch types)
acceptance_criteria:
Acceptance
- Update requests with title/description set to empty/whitespace are rejected with OpError::ValidationFailed.
- Types prevent constructing a patch with empty required fields after parsing.
- Tests cover update rejection and valid trimmed updates.

title: Encode watermark snapshot invariants in repl protocol types
description:
**Problem**
Replication watermarks are encoded as *two separate maps*: `WatermarkMap` (seqs) and `WatermarkHeads` (heads). This lets illegal states exist after parsing:
- seq > 0 with missing head
- seq = 0 with head present
- heads map missing namespace/origin entries that exist in seq map

We then reconstruct `Watermark<K>` at runtime via `watermark_state_from_snapshot` and assume this “can’t be wrong” in downstream logic (`unreachable!` on unknown head). The compiler cannot enforce the invariant, and a malformed peer snapshot or refactor can surface as internal errors or panics.

Key refs:
- `crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/repl/proto.rs:22` — separate map types.
- `crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/repl/session.rs:946` — runtime validation.
- `crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/repl/gap_buffer.rs:293` — `unreachable!` on unknown head.

**Impact**
Repl correctness + robustness risk at protocol boundary. Invalid snapshots can crash or poison state.
design:
**Design (opinionated)**
Represent watermark invariants *in the type* at the protocol boundary.

1) Replace split maps with a per‑origin entry type:
- `struct WireWatermark { seq: Seq0, head: Option<Sha256> }`
- `TryFrom<WireWatermark> for Watermark<K>` enforces:
  - `seq == 0` => `head == None`
  - `seq > 0` => `head == Some`

2) Use a single snapshot map:
- `type WatermarkSnapshotWire = BTreeMap<NamespaceId, BTreeMap<ReplicaId, WireWatermark>>`
- Update `Hello`/`Welcome`/`Ack` to use `WatermarkSnapshotWire` (for durable/applied).

3) Wire compatibility:
- If backward compatibility is required, keep the same on‑wire shape but *decode into* `WireWatermark` and validate immediately. Encoding should never produce invalid pairs.

4) Remove `watermark_state_from_snapshot` from callers.
- Session/gap buffer should operate on `Watermark<K>` directly.
- Eliminate `HeadStatus::Unknown` from the repl/session path or keep it strictly internal.

This makes snapshot invariants unrepresentable and catches mistakes at compile time.
acceptance_criteria:
**Acceptance**
- [ ] There is no public API that can create a seq/head snapshot with invalid combinations.
- [ ] `Hello`/`Welcome`/`Ack` decoding fails fast on invalid seq/head pairs.
- [ ] Session/gap buffer code consumes typed `Watermark<K>` directly (no runtime `watermark_state_from_snapshot`).
- [ ] No `unreachable!` remains in repl paths for `HeadStatus::Unknown`.
- [ ] Tests cover invalid snapshots (missing head for seq>0, head present for seq=0) and round‑trip encoding.

description: darin@darins-Mac-Studio-2.local
title: darin@darins-Mac-Studio-2.local
Fix collision resolution: winner can be deleted by resolution tombstone
description:
**Problem**
In `src/git/collision.rs`, collision resolution remaps the loser and writes a tombstone for the old ID using a "resolution stamp". But the merge rule in `core/state.rs` is: tombstone wins unless the beads `updated_stamp()` is strictly newer.

If the tombstone stamp is newer than the winner beads updated stamp (likely, since resolution stamp is generated "now"), **the winner gets deleted during join**. This violates spec §4.1.1 ("winner retains original id") and is catastrophic.

**Design Options**

*Option A (cleanest):* Lineage-scoped tombstones
Tombstone includes the losing beads `created` stamp (or content-hash) so it only kills that lineage, not the winner occupying the ID.

*Option B (minimal change):* Emit two stamps on collision resolution
- `t_del` for the loser-id tombstone  
- `t_win` for a no-op "bump" on the winner bead so `t_win > t_del`
This makes the winner survive the delete rule while preventing loser resurrection.

**Acceptance**
- [ ] Deterministic test: collision where winner is older, loser is newer, resolution happens later
- [ ] Winner survives resolution in all timestamp orderings
- [ ] Loser cannot resurrect after resolution
- [ ] Spec compliance verified

**Files:** src/git/collision.rs, src/core/state.rs
